identity.short_name: Reciprocal quartic triple-square sum -> cubic Newton recurrence partial sum

problem_spec.summary: Composite (CHAIN) family: compute bridge quantity T from component A (Triple products of squared roots via reciprocal Newton sums (quartic)); then use that T as a required parameter in component B (Partial sum of cubic-root power sums modulo 1000) to determine the final output (reduced mod 1000). Instance summary: Compute a reciprocal-quartic symmetric triple-product-of-squares invariant T, then use T as the endpoint in a Newton/Vieta-derived recurrence for cubic-root power sums; output the partial sum mod 1000.
givens: P(x)=x^4-2x^3+x^2-2x+1 is monic; r_1,...,r_4 are its roots.
alpha,beta,gamma are the roots of y^3-3y+1=0; S_n=alpha^n+beta^n+gamma^n.

parameter(s).name: cubic_coeffs
parameter(s).purpose: pins the cubic power-sum recurrence instance

parameter(s).name: degree
parameter(s).purpose: fixes Newton-identity depth and the symmetric-expression arity (triples of 4 roots).

parameter(s).name: coefficients
parameter(s).domain: (a1,a2,a3,a4) \in ℤ^4 for a monic quartic P(x)=x^4+a1 x^3+a2 x^2+a3 x+a4, with a4\neq0
parameter(s).purpose: controls the root symmetric sums; nonzero constant term enables reciprocal-root methods.

parameter(s).name: cubic_coefficients
parameter(s).domain: monic cubic x^3 - s1 x^2 + s2 x - s3 with integer coefficients
parameter(s).purpose: determines the symmetric sums and the induced power-sum recurrence

parameter(s).name: m
parameter(s).purpose: modulus for the final reduction (here m=1000)

parameter(s).name: output_statistic
parameter(s).purpose: output is T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n rather than a single S_N

parameter(s).name: T_bridge
parameter(s).domain: derived integer computed from component A invariants
parameter(s).purpose: interface quantity produced by component A and consumed by component B (non-stapled coupling).

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_root_multiset
invariant(s).statement: Since P is monic with constant term 1, if r is a root then 1/r is a root (with multiplicity).

invariant(s).name: cubic_power_sum_recurrence
invariant(s).statement: From y^3=3y-1, the power sums satisfy S_n=3S_{n-2}-S_{n-3} for n>=3.

invariant(s).name: complementary_product_identity
invariant(s).statement: For roots r1..r4 with product e4=a4, we have \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 = e4^2 \sum_{i=1}^4 1/r_i^2.

invariant(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_roots
invariant(s).statement: If a4\neq0, then s_i=1/r_i are roots of the monic reciprocal polynomial Q(x) = (1/a4) x^4 P(1/x) = x^4 + (a3/a4)x^3 + (a2/a4)x^2 + (a1/a4)x + (1/a4).

invariant(s).name: newton_girard_for_p2
invariant(s).statement: For monic Q(x)=x^4+A1 x^3+A2 x^2+A3 x+A4 with roots s_i and p_k=\sum s_i^k, Newton gives p1=-A1 and p2 = A1^2 - 2A2, uniquely determined by (A1,A2).

invariant(s).name: coefficient_only_closed_form
invariant(s).statement: Combining the reciprocal transform and Newton for p2 yields T = a4^2Â \cdot p2 = a4^2Â \cdot ((a3/a4)^2 - 2(a2/a4)) = a3^2 - 2 a2 a4, so T is a coefficient polynomial independent of a1.

invariant(s).name: partial_sum_determinism
invariant(s).statement: Once S_0,...,S_N are determined, T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n is uniquely determined.

invariant(s).name: bridge_interface_invariant
invariant(s).statement: The composite is coupled by the interface variable T: component A uniquely determines T, and component B is defined/solved only after substituting this specific T into its parameter slot.

essential_assumption(s): All sums are over roots with multiplicity; power sums are well-defined in C.
Constant term a4 \neq 0 so no root is 0 and reciprocals 1/r_i exist.
Roots are counted with multiplicity so symmetric identities apply directly.
Polynomial is monic so Newton-Girard identities use the standard monic form.
The polynomial is monic of degree 3, so every root satisfies the same cubic relation, yielding a linear recurrence for S_n.
Roots are counted with multiplicity (standard for Vieta/Newton sums).
Modular reduction is applied to integer values obtained from the recurrence.
The bridge quantity T computed from component A is guaranteed to be an integer in the admissible range required by component B (e.g., as an index, bound, or group-action parameter).
uniqueness_certificate(s): T is determined uniquely by the quartic coefficients via symmetric polynomials.
Given T, the recurrence/Vieta initial conditions determine the partial sum uniquely; the mod 1000 normalization is deterministic.

The target T is symmetric in the multiset of roots, hence determined uniquely by coefficients. The complementary-product identity reduces T to e4^2 times the power sum p2 of reciprocal roots. Newton-Girard identities compute p2 uniquely from the first two coefficients of the reciprocal polynomial, so T is uniquely determined (equivalently by the closed form T=a3^2-2a2a4).
The recurrence S_n = 3S_{n-2} - S_{n-3} together with initial values S_0=3, S_1=0, S_2=6 uniquely determines S_0,...,S_20, hence uniquely determines T and T mod 1000.
(N1 necessity) If T is not computed from component A, component B has multiple admissible outputs across different possible parameter choices; thus the composite is ill-posed without the bridge.
(N2 non-factorization) The final answer is not a trivial Combine(ansA, ansB): the computed T restricts/changes the admissible branch structure inside component B before its output is even defined.

technique(s).name: reciprocal_root_pairing
technique(s).purpose: convert triple products into reciprocal power sums
outputs: T as a low-degree power sum

technique(s).name: newton_vieta_power_sums
technique(s).purpose: compute p2 and S0,S1,S2 without solving roots

technique(s).name: linear_recurrence_mod_1000
technique(s).purpose: validate/extend S_n and compute sums modulo 1000

technique(s).name: symmetric_rewrite_via_complementary_products
technique(s).purpose: rewrite \sum_{i<j<k} r_i^2 r_j^2 r_k^2 as e4^2\sum 1/r_i^2.
outputs: reduction to a reciprocal-root power sum

technique(s).name: reciprocal_polynomial_transform
technique(s).purpose: produce the monic polynomial Q whose roots are s_i=1/r_i.

technique(s).name: Newton_identities_computation_for_p2
technique(s).purpose: compute p2=\sum s_i^2 using Newton-Girard (only up to k=2 is needed).

technique(s).name: coefficient_substitution_and_simplification
technique(s).purpose: obtain a closed-form coefficient formula and evaluate for the given polynomial.

technique(s).name: vieta_formulas_to_get_initial_power_sums
technique(s).purpose: compute S_0,S_1,S_2 from the elementary symmetric sums

technique(s).name: linear_recurrence_from_root_equation
technique(s).purpose: derive a constant-coefficient recurrence for S_n by multiplying r^3=3r-1 by r^{n-3} and summing

technique(s).name: iterative_recurrence_evaluation
technique(s).purpose: compute S_0,...,S_N (optionally reducing mod m along the way)

technique(s).name: partial_sum_modular_accumulation
technique(s).purpose: compute T=\sum_{n=0}^N S_n and reduce modulo m

technique(s).name: modular_reduction_final
technique(s).purpose: present the final residue class representative in {0,1,...,m-1}

theorem(s).name: Vieta_formulas
theorem(s).role: relate elementary symmetric sums to coefficients

theorem(s).name: Newton_identities
theorem(s).role: relate power sums to elementary symmetric sums

theorem(s).name: Newton-Girard identities
theorem(s).role: relate power sums of roots of Q to its coefficients, determining p2 uniquely.

theorem(s).name: Vieta formulas
theorem(s).role: identify e4 as the constant term of P and justify symmetric dependence on coefficients.

theorem(s).name: Fundamental theorem of symmetric polynomials
theorem(s).role: guarantees any symmetric expression in roots is a polynomial in coefficients (uniqueness of value).

theorem(s).name: Newton_sums_identities
theorem(s).role: justifies deriving linear recurrences for power sums from the polynomial satisfied by the roots

closest_first_principle_concept_applied: Symmetric expressions in roots are determined by coefficients; invert-roots via reciprocal polynomials to access reciprocal power sums.
Linear dependence of powers of a root modulo its defining polynomial induces a recurrence for symmetric power sums.

critical_trick(s): Convert the quartic triple-square sum into a reciprocal-square power sum using \prod r_i=1.
Use Newton/Vieta to compute the needed power sums without explicitly finding roots.
Bridge the computed T into the cubic power-sum endpoint.
Bridge Lemma (explicit): compute T in component A, then substitute this specific T into component B; justify N1 and N2 to rule out stapling.

library(s).name: math
library(s).reason: basic modular arithmetic and combinatorial counts

task(s).name: primary_structural_check
task(s).reason: Execute the intended structural route in a CAS-friendly way: compute bridge T from component A using symbolic identities or explicit enumeration, then compute component B output with the substituted T, finally reduce mod 1000; confirm matches the recorded Answer.

task(s).name: secondary_independent_check
task(s).reason: Independent cross-check: avoid reusing the same derivation as primary. Use a different route (e.g., brute-force enumeration over the finite search space, numeric root approximation with direct evaluation, or an alternate invariant/Burnside count) to recompute the final answer and compare.
